name: Starter Pack
description: 20 fascinating facts across science, history, psychology, and technology.

items.promptText: The least competent people are often the most confident — this is the Dunning-Kruger effect.
items.explanationText: Dunning and Kruger (1999) found that people with low competence lack the metacognitive ability to recognise their own incompetence. Highly skilled people often underestimate their ability by comparison.

items.promptText: Every time you recall a memory, you subtly rewrite it.
items.explanationText: Memory reconsolidation: retrieving a memory makes it temporarily unstable. The brain re-saves it, and during this window it can be altered. This is why eyewitness testimony is unreliable and why therapy can reframe traumatic memories.

items.promptText: In Asch's experiments, 75% of people gave an obviously wrong answer just to conform with the group.
items.explanationText: Solomon Asch (1951) showed that social pressure can override clear perceptual evidence. When confederates gave wrong answers, most participants conformed rather than contradict the group — a powerful demonstration of conformity.

items.promptText: Over 90% of cyberattacks start with a phishing email.
items.explanationText: Phishing exploits human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities. Attackers impersonate trusted entities to steal credentials or deploy malware. Email filtering and security awareness training are the primary defences.

items.promptText: Large Language Models don't 'think' — they predict the most statistically likely next token.
items.explanationText: LLMs like GPT are statistical models trained on token probabilities. They have no understanding, goals, or internal world model. This makes them impressive at language tasks but brittle outside their training distribution.

items.promptText: Moore's Law: the number of transistors in a chip roughly doubles every two years.
items.explanationText: Gordon Moore (Intel) observed in 1965 that transistor density doubled annually; he later revised this to ~2 years. This drove exponential growth in computing power for 50+ years, though the trend is now slowing as we approach physical limits.

items.promptText: Nothing can travel faster than light — 299,792 km/s in a vacuum.
items.explanationText: Einstein's special relativity shows that as an object approaches the speed of light, its effective mass increases toward infinity, requiring infinite energy. The speed of light is therefore an absolute cosmic speed limit.

items.promptText: CRISPR-Cas9 lets scientists edit DNA like a word processor — cut, paste, replace.
items.explanationText: CRISPR was discovered in bacteria as an immune mechanism. Scientists adapted it into a precise gene-editing tool. Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier won the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for this breakthrough.

items.promptText: The 'fall' of Rome in 476 AD was only the western half — the Eastern Empire survived until 1453.
items.explanationText: The Eastern Roman Empire (Byzantine Empire) continued for nearly 1,000 years after the west fell. It preserved Greek and Roman knowledge and fell only when Constantinople was conquered by the Ottoman Turks in 1453.

items.promptText: The internet began as ARPANET in 1969 — a military network designed to survive a nuclear attack.
items.explanationText: ARPANET connected four US university computers in 1969. Its decentralised packet-switching design meant no single node failure could bring it down — a key military requirement. It later evolved into the civilian Internet.

items.promptText: Mitochondria have their own DNA — evidence they were once free-living bacteria.
items.explanationText: The endosymbiotic theory (Lynn Margulis, 1967) proposes that mitochondria were originally α-proteobacteria engulfed by early cells ~2 billion years ago. Their own circular DNA, ribosomes, and double membrane are evidence of this ancient partnership.
options: Produce DNA for the whole cell

items.promptText: An estimated 99% of all species that have ever lived on Earth are now extinct.
items.explanationText: Life on Earth has endured at least 5 mass extinctions. The fossil record shows species typically survive 1–10 million years before extinction or evolution into new forms. Current extinction rates are estimated at 100–1,000x the natural background rate.

items.promptText: "Inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon." — Milton Friedman
items.explanationText: Friedman's monetarist view holds that sustained inflation results from excessive growth of the money supply. While widely influential, modern economists also recognise supply shocks and expectations as important drivers.

items.promptText: Losing €100 feels about twice as painful as gaining €100 feels good — this is loss aversion.
items.explanationText: Kahneman and Tversky's Prospect Theory (1979) showed that the pain of losses is roughly 2x the pleasure of equivalent gains. This explains irrational risk-averse choices and why 'no-risk' or 'free' offers are so psychologically powerful.

items.promptText: When you look at a distant star, you're seeing the past — its light left thousands of years ago.
items.explanationText: The nearest star (Proxima Centauri) is 4.24 light-years away, so we see it as it was 4 years ago. Some visible stars are thousands of light-years distant. Observing the farthest galaxies means seeing them as they were 13+ billion years ago.

items.promptText: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies.
items.explanationText: A 2016 study (Conselice et al.) used Hubble Deep Field data to extrapolate the total galaxy count to ~2 trillion — 10x the previous estimate of 200 billion. Each galaxy contains billions to trillions of stars.

items.promptText: Water's unusually high boiling point exists because of hydrogen bonds between molecules.
items.explanationText: H₂O molecules form hydrogen bonds due to oxygen's high electronegativity. These bonds require more energy to break — hence water boils at 100°C, far higher than similar-sized molecules like H₂S (-60°C). This wide liquid range enables life on Earth.

items.promptText: Schrödinger's cat illustrates that quantum particles exist in superposition until observed.
items.explanationText: Erwin Schrödinger's 1935 thought experiment exposes the paradox of quantum superposition at macro scale. A cat in a sealed box is 'both alive and dead' until observed — highlighting the measurement problem in quantum mechanics.

items.promptText: After puberty, it becomes very hard to learn a new language without a foreign accent.
items.explanationText: The 'critical period hypothesis' (Lenneberg, 1967) proposes a developmental window for native-like language acquisition. After puberty, neuroplasticity decreases and phonemic categories from the native language are fixed — making accent-free learning rare but not impossible.

items.promptText: The Impressionists were rejected from the official Paris Salon and founded their own independent exhibition.
items.explanationText: In 1874, Monet, Renoir, Degas, and others held their first independent exhibition after rejection by the Académie des Beaux-Arts. A critic mockingly used 'Impressionism' (from Monet's 'Impression, Sunrise') as an insult — the artists proudly adopted the name.

title: Starter Pack
